title: Uragoga microcarpa
taxonomy: Uragoga microcarpa is classified within the family Apocynaceae. This classification places it within the order Gentianales, which includes various flowering plants. The family Apocynaceae is a large group of plants that also includes the milkweed and periwinkle genera. Uragoga microcarpa is a species within this family, and its classification is further supported by its shared characteristics with other Apocynaceae members. Its classification helps to understand its evolutionary relationships and position within the plant kingdom.
uses: Uragoga microcarpa is a plant species that has been traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties. Uragoga microcarpa is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, which may help in treating wounds, infections, and skin conditions. The plant has also been used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and stomach ulcers. Furthermore, Uragoga microcarpa is thought to have antioxidant properties, which may help in protecting against cell damage and oxidative stress. More research is needed to fully understand the potential health benefits of Uragoga microcarpa and its traditional uses.

constituents: Uragoga microcarpa, also known as the 'pumpkin leaf' or 'pumpkin bush', contains various active constituents that contribute to its medicinal properties. The plant is rich in flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which have been identified as its primary bioactive compounds. Flavonoids such as quercetin and kaempferol are present in the plant and are known for their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Additionally, alkaloids like berberine and palmatine have been isolated from Uragoga microcarpa, which have been reported to possess antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activities. The specific profile of active constituents in Uragoga microcarpa may vary depending on factors such as the plant's geographical origin and method of preparation.
